exemplar.title: A Semi-Replication of the Sparrow et al. (2011) Google Effect Study

detailedExemplarData.title: A Semi-Replication of the Sparrow et al. (2011) Google Effect Study

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, focused aim with explicit educational relevance
Well-stated directional and null hypotheses with operationalized IV and DV
Theory/model identified and linked to investigation
weaknesses: Transactive Memory System description lacks depth on encoding/storage processes
Theoretical link to digital reliance is asserted but not critically examined
Minor wording issues in hypothesis regarding âreliance on technologyâ
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Introduction strands each met top-band descriptors but exhibited superficial theoretical detail and minor phrasing issues. The aim, relevance and hypothesis were clearly presented, but deeper explanation of TMS and stronger operational definitions would be needed for full marks. A mid-band mark of 4 reflects solid performance with room for more conceptual depth.

strengths: Thorough explanation of independent-measures design choice (order effects, bias)
Sampling rationale includes appropriateness and limitations analysis
Controlled variables and materials explained with clear justification
weaknesses: Demographic data (e.g., cultural percentages) missing to support sampling claims
Randomisation procedure not fully detailed (software, seed)
Some tables use unexplained symbols and inconsistent layout, reducing clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three Exploration strands fully satisfy the âexplainedâ descriptors, demonstrating strong methodological reasoning. Minor omissions in demographic support, formatting and randomisation detail prevent perfection, but overall the design, sampling and controls are clearly justified, warranting full marks (4/4).

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive and inferential statistics (t-test)
Graphical presentation directly addressed hypothesis with correct labelling
Interpretation linked statistical outcome to hypothesis decision
weaknesses: Descriptive tables contained inconsistent rounding and unclear percentile grouping
No formal analysis of potential correlation between memory level and recall
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis demonstrates correct choice of statistics and effective graphing/interpretation, but notable inconsistencies and reporting errors in tables, plus missing supplementary analyses, limit the quality. This places the work solidly in the mid-band for Analysis, meriting 3/6.

strengths: Findings discussed with reference to Google effect and TMS theory
Strengths and limitations are explicitly linked to validity and generalisability
Modifications tied to specific limitations with clear justifications
weaknesses: Discussion remains surface-level and sometimes speculative (Type II error considerations missing)
Overstatement of theoretical conclusions based on non-significant result
Repetition in sample bias discussion rather than practical implementation details
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation strands meet âdescribed and linkedâ criteria, showing explicit connections between findings, theory, limitations and improvements. However, overstatements, speculative elements and some repetition reduce depth. This supports a mid-band score of 3/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart with error bars compares mean recall across conditions, directly addressing the hypothesis, and is correctly labelled.  Minor omissions (no exact axis titles) prevent full perfection but accuracy is high, so a 5 within the top band is justified.

The student clearly states the statistical outcome (p = 0.11), concludes the difference is not significant, and **links this interpretation to acceptance of the null hypothesis**.  The reasoning refers back to the data and hypothesis, satisfying the 5-6 descriptor; depth is adequate but not exceptional, so 5 is awarded.

A directional research hypothesis specifies the IV (saved vs non-saved file condition) and the DV (number of trivia facts recalled).  Both variables are **clearly operationalised** (binary condition; recall scored 0-8).  The null hypothesis is also included.  This meets all requirements for the top band; minor wording issues justify a 5 rather than 6.

Convenience sampling is described and **its appropriateness and limitations are analysed** (age, culture, use of psychology students, generalisability).  The strand therefore meets the âexplainedâ requirement; the discussion is clear but not exceptionally sophisticated, meriting 4.

Transactive Memory System theory is **accurately outlined** and explicitly linked to the Google effect and to the planned manipulation (information saved vs not saved).  The description is coherent, and the connection to the investigation is spelled out.  Some conceptual detail is thin, but the criterion of âdescribed and link explainedâ is fully met; a mark of 5 reflects solid, if not exceptional, coverage.

Findings are tied to the Google effect and transactive memory theory, noting divergence from Sparrow et al.  The discussion touches on implications for memory models, but the analysis is relatively surface-level and sometimes speculative.  This fits the 3-4 band, at the top end (4).

The student states a clear aim â to investigate whether the Google effect occurs among high-school students â and then **explains the educational relevance** (implications for curricula, technology integration and future research).  The explanation is brief but explicit and goes beyond merely identifying relevance, satisfying the 5-6 descriptor, though the depth is not outstanding, so a mid-band mark (5) is appropriate.

Multiple strengths (controlled setting, independent measures) and limitations (small sample, psychology-student bias, non-standardised memory test) are **explicitly explained and linked** to validity and generalisability, demonstrating relevance to the investigation.  Coverage is detailed though not exhaustive; a 5 reflects strong performance within the top band.

Controlled variables are tabulated with rationales, and materials are justified (e.g., standardised instructions to reduce bias).  This goes beyond mere listing, providing **explicit explanations** of why and how each control was used, matching the 3-4 band.  Quality is solid, so 4 is assigned.

Suggested improvements (increase sample size, random sampling, standardised memory test, quieter setting) are each **directly connected to stated limitations** and include clear justifications (e.g., greater statistical power).  This meets the requirement for explicit linkage and full justification, warranting placement in the 5-6 band; depth is solid but not comprehensive, so 5 is appropriate.

The student not only describes the independent-measures design but **explains why it was chosen over repeated and matched-pairs**, referring to order effects and participant bias.  This reasoning fulfils the âexplainedâ descriptor, so the strand sits at 3-4; the explanation is competent, warranting the top of that band (4).

Descriptive statistics (means, SDs) and an independent-samples t-test are presented and appropriate for the design.  However, tables show some inconsistencies (e.g., mixed denominators, unclear percentile aggregation), indicating **possible calculation/ reporting errors**.  This aligns with the 3-4 band; a 4 reflects mostly correct but slightly flawed application.

annotations.comment: The international school point is valid but should be supported by demographic data showing multicultural representation percentages.
Participant characteristics list is comprehensive. Age, gender, grades, and fluency are all addressed.
The mention of psychology studentsâ familiarity with experiments is good but lacks depth on how this could systematically bias recall.
The fluency criterion mentions Ï but this symbol is unexplained and distracts. Either remove or define any statistical symbols used.
Measured variables table clearly outlines IV and DV, with operational methods specified. This clarity strengthens the methodological rigour.
The table uses colspan and inline line breaks inconsistently, which obscures some variable descriptions. Simplify the layout for readability and ensure each cell aligns with its header.
Controlled variables table provides rationale but often in generic terms. Tie each control directly to potential memoryârecall confounds to reinforce validity.
Uncontrolled variables table correctly identifies residual confounds. For 'participant energy', consider including a brief sleepâquality selfâreport to quantify this variable.
For 'differences in knowledge', propose adding a preâtest of trivia familiarity to control this confound rather than assuming uniform prior exposure.

The description of the Transactive Memory System is superficial. Key processes such as encoding, storage, and retrieval roles within group contexts are missing. The student should deepen the theoretical explanation to show how TSM operates in digital memory reliance.
The link between TSM and the Google effect is asserted but not critically explained. Consider elaborating how relying on external digital stores specifically undermines individual encoding processes.
The aim is clearly stated and its relevance to curriculum design is noted. To strengthen, the student might tie expected educational outcomes to specific pedagogical strategies.
The proposed baseline memory test is mentioned but not fully operationalized. Specify the testâs reliability, scoring criteria, and how percentiles will be computed before grouping participants.
The research hypothesis clearly states IV and DV but omits operational definitions for âreliance on technology.â Suggest defining what scores or behaviors constitute reliance.
Justification for independent-measures design is solid. The student explains order effect avoidance and participant bias reduction effectively.
The description states random allocation but lacks detail on the randomisation procedure (e.g., software used, seed). Include this for replicability.
The matched-pairs design rationale is incomplete and somewhat inaccurate. Matched-pairs could control baseline memory differences if implemented properly; clarify why it was inappropriate here.
The sampling justification references school size but could discuss power analysis or minimum sample for t-test assumptions.
The inclusion of a baseline recall test in materials is appropriate. The student justifies its use for grouping memory levels well.
The description of Quizlet modes uses symbols like \(\mathcal{L}^{\pm}\) without explanation. Clarify each modeâs functionality and relevance.
The percentileâbased grouping in the descriptive table obscures direct comparison of raw means. Present overall group means and SDs for clarity.
Standard deviations in the non-save table are inconsistently rounded. Ensure consistent decimal places and verify calculations to avoid reporting errors.
Bar chart is well labelled with means and error bars, directly addressing the hypothesis. This graphical clarity supports data interpretation.
The claim that no relationship exists between memory level and recall is unsupported by a formal correlation analysis. Suggest conducting and reporting Pearsonâs r.
Discussion notes participant sample differences but could strengthen by referencing Sparrow et al.âs original effect sizes and demographic contrasts.
Concluding that TSM âdoesn't play a big partâ overstates the null finding. Recommend discussing statistical power and the possibility of a Type II error.
Proposed natural experiment to improve ecological validity is promising; consider specifying context (e.g., classroom vs home) to guide future studies.
The sample bias discussion is repeated in Evaluation. Instead, focus here on how random sampling could be implemented in practice (e.g., lottery system).
The suggestion to add qualitative methods is strong. To build on this, propose specific interview questions targeting participantsâ feelings of reliance on technology.
